title: Dyeing with St John's Wort
subtitle: Using Hypericum perforatum flowers and buds with alum mordant to dye wool a warm gold-yellow, with an optional iron shift to olive-green
excerpt: St John's Wort flowers picked just before full bloom give alum-mordanted wool a warm gold-yellow. An iron after-bath shifts the result to olive-green. The dye comes entirely from the yellow flowers and buds; leaves and stems contribute little colour. This entry covers the mordant bath, the flower decoction, the dye bath, and the optional iron shift.
sourceNotes: Pellew, Charles Livingston. Dyes and Dyeing. New York, 1913. Public domain. Internet Archive. Hummel, J. J. The Dyeing of Textile Fabrics. London, 1885. Public domain. Internet Archive. Both sources document Hypericum species colour range on alum- and iron-mordanted wool.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt or mineral compound that bonds to both the fibre and the dye molecule, creating a stable bridge between them. Without a mordant, most plant dyes wash out quickly. Alum potash is the most widely used mordant for wool and gives clear, warm results with most plant dyes.

The liquid in which dyed fibre is immersed to take up colour. The dye bath is prepared by simmering or steeping plant material in water, straining out the solids, and using the remaining dye-rich liquid. Temperature, pH, and time in the bath all affect the final colour on the fibre.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Dye-pots are dedicated and never return to food use. Use stainless steel or enamelled pots. Wear gloves and apron. Work with extractor running or outdoors.

content.text: St John's Wort (Hypericum perforatum) gives alum-

wool a warm gold-yellow. Flowers picked just before full bloom contain the highest concentration of dye compounds: hypericin and flavonoids in the petals and buds. By the time the flowers are fully open the dye content has already begun to decline. Leaves and stems contribute very little colour and are best left out of the dye pot.

The gold-yellow from alum mordant is characteristic of the flavonoid class: warm, slightly orange-edged, and reasonably light-fast on wool. An optional iron after-bath shifts the gold toward an olive-green. The colour shift with iron is pronounced with St John's Wort, moving clearly away from yellow into the olive-to-khaki range.

items.name: Wool yarn or fleece, dry weight
St John's Wort flowers and buds, freshly picked (100% weight of fibre)
Iron sulphate modifier, optional (2% weight of fibre)
Stainless steel dye-pot, dedicated to dyeing
Fine-mesh sieve or muslin for straining

content.text: Submerge the wool in cool water and leave for at least 30 minutes before the mordant bath. Pre-wetting ensures even mordant uptake. Squeeze gently without wringing before moving the wool to the mordant bath.

Dissolve 25 g of alum potash in 200 ml of hot water, then stir into a pot of warm water large enough for the wool to move freely. Lower the pre-wetted wool in. Bring to 80°C over 30 minutes and hold at 80°C for 45 minutes, turning the wool every 10 minutes. Remove and set aside without rinsing. The mordant bath can be stored for one further use once cooled.

Place the flowers and buds in the dye-pot and cover with water. Bring to 80°C and hold for 45 minutes, pressing the flowers occasionally. The bath will turn a golden-yellow. Strain through a fine-mesh sieve, pressing the spent flowers firmly. Discard the plant material. Return the golden liquid to the pot.
Lower the mordanted wool into the strained

. Bring to 80°C over 20 minutes and hold for 60 minutes, turning the wool every 15 minutes. Lift a small section at 45 minutes and rinse quickly under cool water to check the colour: a warm gold-yellow on alum-mordanted wool is the expected result. Remove once the colour is where you want it.

Lift the wool from the dye bath and cool slightly. Rinse in water at a similar temperature to avoid felting. Rinse until the water runs clear. Squeeze gently without wringing. Dry in shade. St John's Wort yellow on alum-mordanted wool is reasonably light-fast; expect gradual softening over years in strong light.

To shift the gold-yellow to olive-green, dissolve 2 g of iron sulphate in 200 ml of hot water, stir into a separate pot of warm water at 60°C, and lower the rinsed dyed wool in. Hold at 60°C for 10 to 15 minutes, turning once. The shift is pronounced: from warm gold to a muted olive-khaki. Remove as soon as the colour is where you want it. Rinse in water at the same temperature until clear, then dry in shade.

Alum potash (25% weight of fibre) gives the base warm gold-yellow on wool. Copper sulphate mordant (2% weight of fibre, used in place of alum) gives a softer, more greenish gold. Iron mordant used from the start rather than as an after-bath gives a deeper olive-brown than the after-bath route. On unmordanted wool the bath gives a pale straw-yellow.

items.problem: Colour came out pale straw rather than gold
items.cause: Flowers were past their peak bloom, or insufficient plant material was used
items.fix: Use flowers picked just before fully open. Increase to 150% weight of fibre if the flowers are old or dried rather than fresh. Fresh flowers give the most reliable result.

items.problem: Colour is uneven with blotchy patches
items.cause: Wool was not pre-wetted, or was lowered into a hot bath rather than a warm one
items.fix: Pre-wet the wool for at least 30 minutes and lower it into a warm bath before slowly raising the temperature to 80°C.

items.problem: Iron after-bath gave a grey-brown rather than olive
items.cause: Too much iron sulphate or the after-bath temperature was too high
items.fix: Use no more than 2% weight of fibre iron sulphate and keep the after-bath at 60°C rather than 80°C. Over-ironing deadens the colour rather than greening it.

content.text: The spent dye bath and alum mordant bath are low-hazard at home quantities: both can go down a foul drain once cooled. The spent plant material is organic; it can go in a food-waste bin or garden compost. If the iron after-bath option was used, the iron sulphate bath must go down a foul drain only. Never dispose of iron bath water into a storm drain, watercourse, soakaway, or garden: iron sulphate is harmful to aquatic invertebrates at low concentrations.
